data.title: Australian Government Halves Fuel Excise Amid Price Surge
data.description:
By James Glynn


SYDNEY--The Australian government will sharply reduce federal taxes on road use and fuel costs to cushion the impact on consumers as gasoline prices surge amid the ongoing war in Iran.

Following a meeting in Canberra of federal, state and territory leaders, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese announced the so-called fuel excise would be cut in half for three months, while other charges currently levied on heavy transport will be scrapped for the same period.

The changes will cost the federal budget 2.55 billion Australian dollars, and will take effect Wednesday, the government said Monday.

Australians are dealing with steep increases in fuel costs across the country, with some economists warnings of a sharp hit to economic growth and a coming spike in inflation.


Write to James Glynn at james.glynn@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-29-26 2238ET

data.title: CMOC Group's Profit Jumps 50% in 2025
Rupee set to climb amid arbitrage unwinding prompted by RBI position limits
data.description:
MUMBAI, March 30 (Reuters) - The Indian rupee is poised to rally at the open on Monday amid arbitrage unwinding prompted by the central bank's curbs on onshore position limits. 

 The Reserve Bank of India late Friday directed banks to cap their net open rupee positions in the foreign exchange market at $100 million by the end of each business day, with compliance required latest by April 10.

The RBI's curbs on onshore position limits are expected to lead to dollar selling by banks in the domestic foreign exchange market amid unwinding of existing arbitrage positions.

These arbitrage trades were built by buying dollars onshore and selling them in the NDF market to exploit the spread between the two segments. 

This spread had widened significantly amid a pickup in volatility and the fall in rupee on heightened risk aversion and oil-driven pressures linked to the Iran war. 

The size of such positions is estimated at $25 billion to over $50 billion.

The pressure on the rupee has been intense amid persistent portfolio outflows and mounting concerns over the impact of higher oil prices on India's economic outlook.

The rupee has dropped more than 4% in March through Friday, putting it on course for its worst monthly performance in more than seven years. 

The currency on Friday dropped nearly 1% to 94.8125, hitting an all-time low of 94.8400.

The RBI had been intervening in both the onshore and NDF markets to support the currency and slow the pace of the rupee's decline. 

"Expect opening quotes to be all over the place--very wide spreads, very choppy price action," a currency trader at a small private sector bank said.

"Any initial dip (in dollar/rupee) should see importer bids come in pretty quickly, while banks keep cutting positions. Hard to call where this settles."

The bid-offer spread on the interbank order system was extremely wide, quoted at 92.00/93.00.

The trader added that banks are likely to incur significant losses since they unwind positions at much wider spreads than where they were initiated.

(Reporting by Nimesh Vora; Editing by Harikrishnan Nair)

By Nimesh Vora

Japan Steps Up Yen Warnings as Mideast War Stokes Inflation Concerns--Update
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa


TOKYO--Bank of Japan Gov. Kazuo Ueda joined a growing chorus of officials pledging to monitor the yen closely, as the Middle East conflict continues to pressure the currency and stoke inflation concerns.

"Exchange-rate fluctuations are one of the key factors that significantly affect our nation's economy and price conditions," Ueda told parliament on Monday.

The BOJ governor reiterated that the central bank is aware that currency swings are becoming more likely to influence prices, as Japanese companies grow more comfortable passing on higher costs to consumers.

"We should pay careful attention to the fact that [currency moves] could also affect underlying inflation through changes in inflation expectations," he said.

Also on Monday, Japan's top currency diplomat, Atsushi Mimura, intensified his rhetoric against speculative moves driving the yen's sharp decline.

He suggested the possibility of government operations to support the currency, which has recently stood near the 160 intervention danger zone against the dollar as safe-haven demand bolsters the greenback.

"There have been observations of increased speculative activity not only in the crude oil futures market but also in the foreign exchange market. If this persists, I believe we will soon need to take decisive action," Mimura said.

While the BOJ left its policy rate unchanged at 0.75% earlier this month, a summary of opinions from that meeting released Monday showed the policy board already on edge.

One member warned that the bank may be forced to speed up the pace of interest-rate hikes if geopolitical risks persist, the summary showed.

"With the policy interest rate still far away from the neutral interest rate, falling behind the curve would compel the bank to pursue rapid and significant monetary tightening, which would cause a major shock to Japan's economy," one BOJ board member said.

The BOJ's summary doesn't identify speakers.

In his parliamentary testimony, Ueda indicated that a steady policy normalization would ultimately help anchor the market.

"We believe that by raising short-term rates at an appropriate pace in line with the rising probability of reaching the 2% inflation target, long-term rates will be established stably and remain consistent with such policy actions," he said.


Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-29-26 2226ET

Eli Lilly, InSilico Strike AI Drug Discovery Deal That Could Be Worth Up to $2.75 Billion
data.description:
By Jason Chau


Eli Lilly has signed a drug discovery deal with InSilico that the Hong Kong-listed biotech firm says could be valued at as much as $2.75 billion.

The partnership is the latest in a string of tie-ups between pharma majors and Chinese firms, and grants Lilly exclusive worldwide rights to a portfolio of programs across multiple therapeutic areas.

InSilico will receive an upfront payment of $115 million, and is eligible for additional milestone payments that could put the total deal value at about $2.75 billion, it said in a filing.

The companies will also work on multiple research-and-development programs focused on targets selected by Lilly, and using InSilico's Pharma.AI platforms.

Shares of InSilico, which specializes in integrating AI and automation in drug discoveries, surged 10% in early trade on Monday.


Write to Jason Chau at jason.chau@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-29-26 2220ET

SoftBank Group to Issue $2.61 Billion Hybrid Notes in Japan
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka


SoftBank Group plans to issue $2.61 billion of hybrid notes in Japan, primarily targeting retail investors.

The Japanese technology investment company said Monday that it would issue 418.0 billion yen of 35-year notes in Japan, to repay previously issued hybrid notes at their first optional redemption date in June.

SoftBank Group said the interest rate on the notes would be fixed for the initial five years and floating thereafter.

The Japanese company said it expected hybrid notes to be eligible for 50% equity treatment from rating companies. The notes are rated at BBB+ by Japan Credit Rating Agency.

The company said detailed terms and conditions are scheduled to be determined on April 10.

SoftBank Group shares were recently 8.4% lower amid broad market selloffs and following sharp declines in shares of chip-design unit Arm Holdings on Friday.


Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-29-26 2210ET
